nativeRange.habitat: Damp ground in fields, lawns, sidewalk cracks, and along stream and pond margins; tolerates occasional submersion and grows readily as a creeping marginal plant

decorPreferences: strong overhead lighting for compact carpeting

avoidWith: plant-eating fish such as goldfish, large cichlids, or silver dollars

summary: Lawn Marshpennywort (Hydrocotyle sibthorpioides), also sold as Pennywort Mini, is a small Asian creeping plant with round to kidney-shaped leaves. It is commonly used as a foreground carpet and tends to stay low and compact under strong light with CO2 supplementation.
careNotes:
Lawn Marshpennywort is a small creeping plant native to southeastern Asia, recognizable by its tiny round-to-kidney-shaped leaves — typically 0.5–2 cm across — with shallowly scalloped edges. It belongs to the family Araliaceae under modern plant taxonomy, which replaced an older placement in Apiaceae. It is worth noting that several other Hydrocotyle species are sold in the aquarium trade under similar names: H. verticillata has larger, half-dollar-sized leaves arranged in whorls; H. leucocephala (Brazilian Pennywort) is a stem plant with larger round leaves; and H. tripartita — sometimes called 'Mini' or 'Japan' — has three-lobed, clover-like leaves. H. sibthorpioides sold under the 'Pennywort Mini' trade name is occasionally confused with H. tripartita 'Mini', so confirming the leaf shape before purchase is worthwhile.

In the aquarium, the plant is most often placed in the foreground or low midground as a carpeting species, spreading by runners that creep across the substrate. Submerged growth is generally reported in the 1–10 cm range; the taller figures sometimes quoted by retailers typically reflect emersed or untrimmed specimens. Strong lighting is generally advised to keep the carpet low and compact — in medium light without CO2 supplementation, the plant tends to grow taller, leggier, and more slowly. CO2 injection at concentrations commonly cited around 20–30 mg/L, combined with a nutrient-rich substrate and regular dosing of nitrate, phosphate, iron, and trace micronutrients, represents the standard approach for achieving a dense, even carpet. Without CO2, the plant is reported to survive under medium light but grows considerably more slowly and is less likely to fill in tightly. Growth rate is generally considered medium overall. A tank of at least 40 L with adequate footprint is recommended to give the carpet room to develop.
breedingNotes: Propagation is straightforward via vegetative means: cut a section of the creeping stem and replant the segment, separate runners or daughter plants from a healthy mother plant, or pull apart and split dense clumps. New shoots can be trimmed off anywhere along a stem and replanted to thicken the carpet.

primary.notes: Manifest-supplied URL for the 'Variegata' product page returned 404; this UNS tissue-culture product page for the non-variegated form on the same retailer was used as the primary source instead.
